en.extract:
The blue-streaked lory (Trichoglossus reticulatus) is also known as the blue-necked lory. It is a medium-sized parrot (31 cm), which is found on the Tanimbar Islands and Babar in the southern Moluccas.


Description
The blue-streaked lory is about 31 centimetres (12 in) in length, including the tail. They weigh between 145 and 155 grams (5.1 and 5.5 oz).
Adults of both sexes look the same – this is called sexually monomorphic.
Most of the bird's general plumage is bright red. Against this intense red background, there are scattered electric blue feathers near the ears and on the nape of the neck, which is where the bird gets its name. From the area to the base of the neck, there is an indigo-violet stripe. The lower part of the nape of the neck, to the top of the back, is a bluish black. The main part of the back, as well as the rump, is red. Wing coverts and flights are red with black tips. The upper part of the tail feathers are a reddish black, and the underside of the tail is a muted red. The iris of the eye is orange-red. The beak is sharply curved, short, and bright red-orange. The feet are medium gray, and the claws are black.


Social ecology
There are estimated to be between 100,000 and 500,000 blue-streaked lories in the wild.
While their diet in the wild is unknown, it is thought to be similar to that of most lories and lorikeets, with pollen, nectar, fruit, and some insects making up the vast bulk of the ingesta.
These lories are known as one of the louder species, and when kept in captivity, people's neighbors who desire quiet might be disturbed or annoyed by the bird's penetrating voice. The call is described as a shrill series of screeches in succession, or singular screeches.
Social and gregarious, these birds travel in noisy flocks of up to ten individuals when possible.
The bird's lifespan in captivity is reported to be 28–32 years.
The usual clutch of eggs is 2, but in exceptional circumstances, 3 eggs may be laid. In commercial aviculture for breeding, it is very important to set up compatible pairs, to prevent harm to one member of the pair.
Incubation time is 25 days, and from the time of hatching, the time to fledge is approximately 12 weeks.


Threats
Main threats to the bird in the wild are habitat degradation (logging, forest extirpation to plant palms or other monocultures) and trapping for the pet trade.
The birds have a restricted and small range, but because the population is not fragmented, it is not severely threatened.
Both primary, old growth forest and replacement, or secondary, forest are preferred habitats. These parrots will live in mangrove forests as well as on human managed lands such as coconut plantations.


Legal status
The IUCN lists these parrots as Near Threatened. For international trade, this parrot is listed as CITES Appendix II, which means that commercial trade must be done with special permits.


Relationship with humans
The bird is tame and playful when they have been raised by humans in captivity. Wild caught birds, being wild, are shy. The bird readily adapts to life in captivity.


Taxonomic note
There are no subspecies; only the nominate race is recognized.


Gallery


References

Juniper & Parr (1998) Parrots: A Guide to Parrots of the World; ISBN 0-300-07453-0.
"Species factsheet: Eos reticulata". BirdLife International (2008). Retrieved 20 March 2009.


External links
Oriental Bird Images: Blue-streaked Lory  Selected photos

The '''blue-streaked lory''' ('''''Trichoglossus reticulatus''''') is also known as the '''blue-necked lory'''. It is a medium-sized [[parrot]] (31 cm), which is found on the [[Tanimbar Islands]] and [[Babar Island|Babar]] in the southern [[Moluccas]].

==Description==

The blue-streaked lory is about {{convert|31|cm|in}} in length, including the tail. They weigh between {{convert|145|and|155|g|oz}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url = https://www.parrots.org/encyclopedia/blue-streaked-lory|title = Blue-streaked Lory (Eos reticulata) {{!}} Parrot Encyclopedia|last = Trust|first = World Parrot|website = www.parrots.org|access-date = 2016-03-04}}</ref>

Adults of both sexes look the same – this is called [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually monomorphic]].<ref name=":0" />

Most of the bird's general [[plumage]] is bright red. Against this intense red background, there are scattered electric blue feathers near the ears and on the nape of the neck, which is where the bird gets its name. From the area to the base of the neck, there is an indigo-violet stripe. The lower part of the nape of the neck, to the top of the back, is a bluish black. The main part of the back, as well as the rump, is red. Wing coverts and flights are red with black tips. The upper part of the tail feathers are a reddish black, and the underside of the tail is a muted red. The iris of the eye is orange-red. The beak is sharply curved, short, and bright red-orange. The feet are medium gray, and the claws are black.<ref name=":0" />

== Social ecology ==

There are estimated to be between 100,000 and 500,000 blue-streaked lories in the wild.<ref name=":0" />

While their diet in the wild is unknown, it is thought to be similar to that of most [[lories and lorikeets]], with pollen, nectar, fruit, and some insects making up the vast bulk of the ingesta.<ref name=":0" />

These lories are known as one of the louder species, and when kept in captivity, people's neighbors who desire quiet might be disturbed or annoyed by the bird's penetrating voice. The call is described as a shrill series of screeches in succession, or singular screeches.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url = http://www.arndt-verlag.com/projekt/birds_3.cgi?Desc=E007.htm&Pic=007_1.JPG|title = Lexicon of Parrots|website = www.arndt-verlag.com|access-date = 2016-03-04}}</ref>

Social and gregarious, these birds travel in noisy flocks of up to ten individuals when possible.<ref name=":0" />

The bird's lifespan in captivity is reported to be 28–32 years.<ref name=":0" />

The usual clutch of eggs is 2, but in exceptional circumstances, 3 eggs may be laid. In commercial aviculture for breeding, it is very important to set up compatible pairs, to prevent harm to one member of the pair.<ref name=":1" />

Incubation time is 25 days, and from the time of hatching, the time to fledge is approximately 12 weeks.<ref name=":0" />

== Threats ==

Main threats to the bird in the wild are habitat degradation (logging, forest extirpation to plant palms or other monocultures) and trapping for the pet trade.<ref name=":1" />

The birds have a restricted and small range, but because the population is not fragmented, it is not severely threatened.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=1342|title = Blue-streaked Lory (Eos reticulata) - BirdLife species factsheet|website = www.birdlife.org|access-date = 2016-03-04}}</ref>

Both primary, old growth forest and replacement, or secondary, forest are preferred habitats. These parrots will live in mangrove forests as well as on human managed lands such as coconut plantations.<ref name=":2" />

== Legal status ==

The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] lists these parrots as Near Threatened. For international trade, this parrot is listed as [[CITES|CITES Appendix II]], which means that commercial trade must be done with special permits.<ref name=":2" />

== Relationship with humans ==

The bird is tame and playful when they have been raised by humans in captivity. Wild caught birds, being wild, are shy. The bird readily adapts to life in captivity.<ref name=":1" />

== Taxonomic note ==

There are no subspecies; only the nominate race is recognized.<ref name=":0" />
